title: Dual role of core electrons in electronic friction
abstract: Non-equilibrium energy dissipation in multi-shell swift-ion/matter systems remains a fundamental yet incompletely understood problem, with electronic stopping power $\mathcal{S}_\text{e}$ as a relevant observable for electronic friction. Using real-time time-dependent density functional theory, we perform first-principles calculations of $\mathcal{S}_\text{e}$ for beryllium self-irradiation with explicit treatment of all electrons. Our results reveal a Bragg peak exhibiting a distinct structure which lies beyond the reach of standard semi-empirical models. We attribute its appearance to a dual effect of the presence of core electrons, by which their excitation provides an additional dissipation channel while simultaneously suppressing valence electron excitations. An electron capture process by the projectile's core from the host cores is behind such suppression, rather than Pauli blocking. This dual mechanism contrasts with the shake-up effect reported for water, whereby the inclusion of core electrons enhances valence excitation. Our work provides a new perspective on the effect and importance of core electrons in projectile energy dissipation in matter.
summary: This paper uses real-time TDDFT to show that core electrons in beryllium both enhance and suppress electronic stopping power. It identifies an electron capture mechanism as the cause for valence excitation suppression, providing a new perspective on energy dissipation in ion-matter interactions that standard semi-empirical models fail to capture.
main_problem: Understanding the mechanism behind the complex, multi-stage Bragg peak structure in electronic stopping power and the dual role of core electrons in energy dissipation.
main_result: Core electrons play a dual role by providing an additional dissipation channel through excitation while simultaneously suppressing valence electron excitations via an electron capture process.
method: Real-time time-dependent density functional theory (rt-TDDFT) using the INQ/QB@LL program with explicit treatment of all electrons.
model_or_system: A beryllium (Be) projectile moving through a bulk beryllium (Be) host, modeled using a 5 x 6 x 3 hexagonal close-packed (hcp) supercell.
key_observables: Electronic stopping power (Se), friction coefficient, Bragg peak structure, and excitation numbers for 1s and 2s electrons.
important_parameters: Velocity regimes (low-velocity, Bragg peak, and high-velocity), atomic number (Z1), and trajectory types (hyper-channeling vs. off-channeling).
main_assumptions: Host atoms are kept frozen to isolate the pure electronic stopping process; use of the PBE exchange-correlation functional; adiabatic approximation in TDDFT.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares stopping power and Bragg peak structure across different trajectories and models; Figure 2 shows the decomposition of stopping power into 1s and 2s components; Figure 3 illustrates excitation distributions and time evolution of excitation events; Supplemental figures demonstrate numerical robustness and energy expectation values.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of energy dissipation, describes the rt-TDDFT methodology and Be system, presents results comparing trajectories and models, analyzes the dual role of core electrons through energy decomposition, and concludes with a discussion on model limitations and future directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Electronic stopping power Se for a Be projectile in bulk Be host as a function of velocity. Filled circles and inverted Y-shape represent Se along the off-channeling and hyper-channeling trajectories, respectively. Skyblue solid and dashed lines show predictions of Se from the SRIM [29] and ESPNN [30] models. Green dotted and dash-dotted lines show Se for a Be projectile in dilute and dense jellium. Blue-green dashed line serves as a guide to the eye indicating the slope change near the Bragg peak in off-channelling. Blue and black dots represent experimental stopping power data for Li in Be [31] and C in Be [32], respectively. The red arrows indicate the positions of Bragg peaks in...

FIG. 2. Electronic stopping power Se (circles and inverted Y- shape) and its decomposed components, 1s (S1s e , green bars) and 2s (S2s e , blue bars), versus projectile velocity along (a) off-channeling and (b) hyper-channeling trajectories.

FIG. 3. Time evolution of the excitation distribution. (a) Excitation distribution P(E) at v = 4 a.u. in off-channeling. Color gradation: lighter colors correspond to larger time; Gaussian broadening σ = 0.8 eV. Blue and green solid lines show P1s(E) and P2s(E) at t = 20 a.u., respectively. (b) Exci- tation number of the 1s bands, N1s. Skyblue peaks (dN1s/dt) indicate core-electron excitation events. Blue dashed line de- notes projectile core ionization. Black dashed line marks the completion of the first pronounced host core excitation. (c) Early-stage excitation number of the 1s (blue) and 2s (green) bands in hyper- and off-channeling (dashed and dotted lines, respectively).

FIG. 4. Four velocity regimes and the screening effect in quasi-valence-electron systems. (a) Stopping power of three quasi-valence-electron systems: the all-electron hyper- channeling case Shyper e , 2s component of the all-electron off- channeling case S2s-off e , and the pseudized off-channeling case SBe2-off e (pseudized host Be atoms and all-electron Be pro- jectile). (b) Electron capture from host cores, indicated by the difference in electron density in off-channeling with all- electron and valence-only (pseudized) host system.
